channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Health Hazard | Cliff Douglas Interview | Part 2 | RegWatch
item.description: Longstanding public health narratives around nicotine are facing renewed scrutiny as tobacco harm reduction challenges older assumptions shaped by the cigarette era. Critics argue that framing nicotine itself as the central danger has contributed to public misunderstanding of reduced-risk products such as vapes, where the main issue is not combustion but the delivery of nicotine without smoke. The discussion raises concerns that fear-based language, including terms such as “epidemic” and misleading comparisons with illicit drugs, may distort risk perception and weaken evidence-based policy. Broader questions remain over whether tobacco control can adapt to scientific evidence on relative risk while maintaining credible public health communication. (Published on May 27, 2026byregulatorwatch.com)

item.title: Teen Vaping Has Long Been Falling. Why Do Schools Think the Opposite?
item.description: Declines in youth vaping in the United States appear to be increasingly at odds with perceptions of an escalating school-based crisis. Official survey data indicate substantial reductions in teen vaping in recent years, while school-level concern remains high due to increased detection, heightened awareness and broader media attention. Researchers and harm reduction advocates warn that this gap between evidence and perception may contribute to policies that restrict adult access to lower-risk nicotine products. The debate highlights the importance of interpreting youth vaping trends carefully, distinguishing between visibility and prevalence, and grounding nicotine regulation in reliable population-level data. (Published on May 27, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: Alaska Legislature passes bill to tax vapes, raise legal tobacco purchase age to 21
item.description: Alaska’s decision to tax vaping products and raise the legal tobacco purchase age to 21 is adding to wider debate over nicotine regulation in the United States. Supporters of the measure frame it as a youth prevention policy, while critics warn that higher costs for reduced-risk alternatives may discourage adult smokers from switching away from combustible cigarettes. The legislation highlights ongoing tensions between taxation, age restrictions, public health messaging and tobacco harm reduction. Questions remain over whether policies aimed at reducing nicotine use broadly can avoid unintended consequences for smokers seeking lower-risk options. (Published on May 27, 2026byadn.com)

item.title: France Is Banning Zyn and Threatening To Jail People for 5 Years
item.description: France’s move to ban ZYN nicotine pouches is intensifying debate over whether prohibition-based regulation can deliver effective public health outcomes. Critics argue that severe penalties for smoke-free nicotine products risk creating a disproportionate framework in which lower-risk alternatives are treated more harshly than combustible cigarettes. The decision raises wider concerns about consumer access, illicit markets and the consistency of nicotine policy across Europe. Questions remain over whether such measures will reduce nicotine use overall or make it harder for adult smokers to move away from the most harmful forms of tobacco. (Published on May 27, 2026byreason.com)

item.title: The Quit-Smoking Tool That’s Beating Patches and Gum
item.description: A major review of global smoking cessation research adds further evidence that nicotine-containing e-cigarettes can help smokers quit more effectively than several commonly used methods, including patches, gum, non-nicotine e-cigarettes and behavioural support alone. The strongest reviews found more consistent benefits for nicotine e-cigarettes, while lower-quality studies produced less certain results. Researchers also highlight remaining evidence gaps, including limited comparisons with some pharmacological treatments and uncertainty around long-term safety outcomes. The findings reinforce the importance of assessing smoking cessation tools through relative risk, real-world effectiveness and the goal of reducing exposure to combustible tobacco. (Published on May 27, 2026byscitechdaily.com)
